items.correct: Unemployment caused by a mismatch between workers' skills/location and available jobs — often due to long-term economic change like deindustrialisation.
items.misconception: Structural unemployment is unemployment caused by a recession.
items.random_wrong: Structural unemployment is when people are too lazy to work.
items.why: Recession-driven unemployment is CYCLICAL. Structural is about long-term mismatches — coal miners can't easily become app developers. Students collapse all unemployment into 'lack of demand'.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: Is a trade deficit necessarily bad?
items.correct: Not necessarily — it can reflect strong domestic demand, attractive investment opportunities, or borrowing to fund growth. Context matters more than the headline figure.
items.misconception: A trade deficit always means a country is losing wealth and failing economically.
items.random_wrong: A trade deficit means a country has no factories.
items.why: The US has run deficits for decades while growing. Trade deficits are balanced by capital inflows. Students treat deficits as automatic failure, missing the accounting identity and what it implies.
Correct answer: Demand falls as price falls. “Vertical supply” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Correct answer: Marshall. “Smith” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year did Marshall's 'Principles of Economics' formalise marginal analysis?
items.why: Exact answer: 1890 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
